title: Chapter 5: The Price of a God
content: Colonel Voss was not having a good apocalypse.
He sat in his commandeered office in the Engineering building, staring at the Nether-Grid deed screen, and tried to understand what he was seeing.
Blackwood University had been purchased. In full. By a twenty-two-year-old art student.
The Coalition's protection contract had been bought out from under him. The Nether-Grid was now showing a new debtor for that territory: Roxanne Vance, not the Salvation Coalition.
And the woman he'd sent to intimidate her — Commander Hayes — had come back with a water purification upgrade and a confused expression.

"She bought our contract," Hayes said, standing in front of his desk with her arms crossed. "Not the Coalition's. Ours. She paid off our generator lease, our water contract, and our Nether-Grid protection fee. All of it."
"How?" Voss's voice was tight. "Where does an art student get that kind of currency?"
"She has a card. A black card. It shows prices for everything."

"A Primordial artifact." The words came out before Voss could stop them. He'd read the old files — the ones the previous administration had kept in the sealed section of the library. Documents about the Hollow Cathedral, the Bound God, and the artifacts that predated human civilization.
"If she has a Primordial artifact, she's connected to the bloodline. The one that was supposed to be extinct."
"That's above my pay grade," Hayes said flatly. "All I know is she paid our debts and asked for nothing except territorial sovereignty. She's not our enemy."
"Yet."
"She could have crushed us. She chose not to. That makes her an ally."
"That makes her unpredictable," Voss said. "And unpredictable is dangerous."
He stood up.
"I want to know everything about her. Her friends. Her history. How she got that card. And I want to know what's in that cathedral basement."
Across campus, Marcus had reached a breaking point.

The Executioner had followed him for sixteen hours straight. It stood outside the bathroom stall while he hid inside. It waited by the door of the storage closet he'd tried to sleep in. It loomed behind him while he desperately tried to find other survivors who would take him in.
No one would. Because the Executioner was with him.
"Please," he said, turning to face the hulking, bandaged figure. "Please. What do you want?"
The Executioner didn't speak. It never spoke. But this time, it raised its chainsaw arm — slowly, deliberately — and dragged the blade across the concrete wall.
Scrape. Scrape. Scrape.
Letters formed in the dust and metal shavings:
*WAIT.*
"Wait for what?"
The Executioner tilted its head. Then it wrote again:
*SHE WILL COME FOR YOU. SHE ALWAYS FINISHES HER PURCHASES.
Marcus felt his blood run cold.
Roxanne sat in the dean's office, looking at the black card in her hands.
One hundred million Obols.
She could afford it. The card said ∞. But buying a god wasn't like buying a campus. A campus was real estate. A god was… a commitment.

Lena was asleep on the leather couch, her sketchbook open on her chest. She'd filled twelve pages with drawings of the Hollow Cathedral in the past three hours — the pillars, the chains, the chained figure at the center.

Roxanne looked at the drawings. Lena had captured something she hadn't noticed at the time: the way the chains weren't just wrapped around Silas. They were growing into him. Fusing with his skin. The seal was eating him alive.
"You're going to buy him, aren't you?"
Lena's voice was sleepy, barely above a whisper.
"I'm thinking about it."
"You've been thinking about it for four hours. You already decided. You're just afraid to admit it."
Roxanne was quiet.
Lena sat up, rubbing her eyes. "He's dangerous, Roxy. You know that. He's a god who was sealed by other gods because they were scared of him. And you're thinking about buying his contract."
"I know."
"He could kill us the second the chains come off."
"He could. But he won't."
"How do you know?"
Roxanne held up the card.
"Because I'll be his contract holder. A Primordial contract can't be broken. If I buy him, he's mine. He can't hurt me. He can't betray me. He can't do anything except what I tell him to do."
Lena stared at her.
"That's not reassuring."
"It's the most reassuring thing in the apocalypse," Roxanne said. "Absolute loyalty. Guaranteed by the fundamental laws of reality."
She stood up.
"I'm going back to the Cathedral. Stay here. The Executioner will guard the building."
"Roxy—"
"If I'm not back in two hours, come find me. But I will be back."
She grabbed her flashlight and walked out the door.
*End of Chapter 5*
Across campus, Marcus had reached a breaking point.

The Executioner had followed him for sixteen hours straight. It stood outside the bathroom stall while he hid inside. It waited by the door of the storage closet he'd tried to sleep in. It loomed behind him while he desperately tried to find other survivors who would take him in.
No one would. Because the Executioner was with him.
"Please," he said, turning to face the hulking, bandaged figure. "Please. What do you want?"
The Executioner didn't speak. It never spoke. But this time, it raised its chainsaw arm — slowly, deliberately — and dragged the blade across the concrete wall.
Scrape. Scrape. Scrape.
Letters formed in the dust and metal shavings:

"Wait for what?"
The Executioner tilted its head. Then it wrote again:
SHE WILL COME FOR YOU. SHE ALWAYS FINISHES HER PURCHASES.
Marcus felt his blood run cold.
Roxanne sat in the dean's office, looking at the black card in her hands.
One hundred million Obols.

She could afford it. The card said ∞. But buying a god wasn't like buying a campus. A campus was real estate. A god was a commitment. A four-billion-year-old commitment with red eyes and questionable intentions.

Lena was asleep on the leather couch, her sketchbook open on her chest. She'd filled twelve pages with drawings of the Hollow Cathedral in the past three hours — the pillars, the chains, the chained figure at the center.

Roxanne looked at the drawings. Lena had captured something she hadn't noticed at the time: the way the chains weren't just wrapped around Silas. They were growing into him. Fusing with his skin. The seal was eating him alive.
"You're going to buy him, aren't you?"
Lena's voice was sleepy, barely above a whisper.
"I'm thinking about it."
"You've been thinking about it for four hours. You already decided. You're just afraid to admit it."
Roxanne was quiet.
Lena sat up, rubbing her eyes. "He's dangerous, Roxy. You know that. He's a god who was sealed by other gods because they were scared of him. And you're thinking about buying his contract."
"I know."
"He could kill us the second the chains come off."
"He could. But he won't."
"How do you know?"
Roxanne held up the card.
"Because I'll be his contract holder. A Primordial contract can't be broken. If I buy him, he's mine. He can't hurt me. He can't betray me. He can't do anything except what I tell him to do."
Lena stared at her.
"That's not reassuring."
"It's the most reassuring thing in the apocalypse," Roxanne said. "Absolute loyalty. Guaranteed by the fundamental laws of reality."
She stood up.
"I'm going back to the Cathedral. Stay here. The Executioner will guard the building."
"Roxy —"
"If I'm not back in two hours, come find me. But I will be back."
She grabbed her flashlight and walked out.
